problem_statement.Title: RidgeClassifierCV does not accept store_cv_values (TypeError on init)
problem_statement.Description: Passing store_cv_values to sklearn.linear_model.RidgeClassifierCV raises TypeError("__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'store_cv_values'"), preventing users from obtaining cv_values_ despite documentation and related classes indicating that RidgeClassifierCV should support storing cross-validation predictions when store_cv_values=True and cv=None.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. RidgeClassifierCV.__init__ (sklearn/linear_model/ridge.py : RidgeClassifierCV.__init__): the constructor signature omits the CV-related parameters gcv_mode and store_cv_values and therefore does not accept or forward store_cv_values from callers. Incorrect assumption encoded: the classifier should not expose the parent's CV-storage parameters. Evidence: current signature only lists alphas, fit_intercept, normalize, scoring, cv, class_weight and calling RidgeClassifierCV(store_cv_values=True) raises TypeError.

2. Missing instance flag prevents downstream behavior (sklearn/linear_model/ridge.py : _BaseRidgeCV.__init__ and _BaseRidgeCV.fit): _BaseRidgeCV expects self.store_cv_values (defaulting to False when present) to decide whether to construct _RidgeGCV with store_cv_values=True and to copy estimator.cv_values_ into self.cv_values_. Because RidgeClassifierCV never sets/forwards store_cv_values, the machinery that produces cv_values_ is not enabled for the classifier. Relationship: when self.store_cv_values is True and cv is None, _BaseRidgeCV.fit constructs _RidgeGCV(..., store_cv_values=self.store_cv_values) and then, if self.store_cv_values, sets self.cv_values_ = estimator.cv_values_.

3. _RidgeGCV computes cv_values_ only when store_cv_values is True (sklearn/linear_model/ridge.py : _RidgeGCV.__init__/fit): the estimator produces estimator.cv_values_ only if store_cv_values=True and cv is None. The shape relationship is preserved: if y is 1D then cv_values_.shape = (n_samples, n_alphas); if y is 2D (n_responses) then cv_values_.shape = (n_samples, n_responses, n_alphas). This formula/shape expectation is relied upon by tests and user documentation.

4. Incompatibility guard is present (sklearn/linear_model/ridge.py : _BaseRidgeCV.fit): _BaseRidgeCV.fit enforces that cv!=None and store_cv_values=True are incompatible by raising ValueError("cv!=None and store_cv_values=True are incompatible"). This guard remains relevant and is part of the expected behavior when store_cv_values is supported.

5. Missing test coverage for classifier (sklearn/linear_model/tests/test_ridge.py): tests exist for RidgeCV(store_cv_values=True) but no equivalent unit test exists for RidgeClassifierCV, so the API omission was not caught by the test suite. Evidence: test_ridgecv_store_cv_values asserts cv_values_.shape for RidgeCV but no parallel test for RidgeClassifierCV was found.
StepsToReproduce: from sklearn import linear_model as lm
lm.RidgeClassifierCV(alphas = np.arange(0.1, 1000, 0.1), normalize = True, store_cv_values = True).fit(x, y)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: RidgeClassifierCV accepts store_cv_values as a boolean constructor parameter. When called with store_cv_values=True and cv=None and after fit(...), the estimator exposes a cv_values_ attribute containing cross-validation predictions with shape (n_samples, n_alphas) for 1D y or (n_samples, n_responses, n_alphas) for 2D y. No TypeError should occur at initialization.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling RidgeClassifierCV(..., store_cv_values=True) raises TypeError("__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'store_cv_values'") and the classifier does not provide a cv_values_ attribute after fit.

problem_statement.Title: LabelEncoder.transform raises TypeError for empty input when fitted with string labels
problem_statement.Description: Calling LabelEncoder.transform([]) raises a TypeError when the encoder was fitted with string labels (or any classes_ dtype incompatible with numpy's default dtype for an empty array). This occurs in sklearn/preprocessing/label.py::LabelEncoder.transform when transforming an empty input.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Incorrect assumption in sklearn/preprocessing/label.py::LabelEncoder.transform: code assumes calling np.searchsorted(self.classes_, y) is safe for empty y. The method does: y = column_or_1d(y, warn=True) followed by return np.searchsorted(self.classes_, y). When y is an empty Python list, column_or_1d produces np.array([], dtype=float64), and numpy attempts to cast that float64 empty array to self.classes_.dtype (e.g. '<U32'), causing the observed TypeError: "Cannot cast array data from dtype('float64') to dtype('<U32') according to the rule 'safe'". The transform implementation contains no special-case for y.size == 0. 2) Incorrect assumption in sklearn/utils/validation.py::column_or_1d: the function returns np.ravel(y) for empty lists, producing an empty array with dtype float64 and assuming downstream code will tolerate that dtype. This behavior results in an empty float64 array being passed into dtype-sensitive operations (such as searchsorted against string-typed classes_), triggering the casting error.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder; le = LabelEncoder(); le.fit([1,2]); print(le.transform([]))"
python -c "from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder; le = LabelEncoder(); le.fit(['a','b']); print(le.transform([]))"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Transforming an empty sequence returns an empty integer array of encoded labels in all cases (e.g. array([], dtype=int)), regardless of the dtype of classes_ learned during fit.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When LabelEncoder was fitted with string labels, calling transform([]) raises a TypeError from numpy (Cannot cast array data from dtype('float64') to dtype('<U32') according to the rule 'safe'), with the traceback pointing to sklearn/preprocessing/label.py line that calls np.searchsorted(self.classes_, y). When fitted with numeric labels, transform([]) returns an empty integer array as expected.

problem_statement.Title: check_array(..., warn_on_dtype=True) does not warn for pandas.DataFrame inputs
problem_statement.Description: When check_array is called with warn_on_dtype=True and the input is a pandas.DataFrame (e.g. dtype=object), no DataConversionWarning is emitted even though the data is converted to a numpy dtype (e.g. float64). The missing warning occurs inside sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_array.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: getattr(array, "dtype") is a numpy.dtype (has a .kind attribute). Location: sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_array (dtype_orig detection block). Effect: for pandas.DataFrame inputs this sets dtype_orig = None because DataFrame.dtypes is a per-column Series, so dtype_orig is discarded.

2) Conditional that emits the warning depends on dtype_orig being non-None. Location: sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_array (warn_on_dtype conditional). Exact conditional used: if warn_on_dtype and dtype_orig is not None and array.dtype != dtype_orig: warnings.warn(..., DataConversionWarning). Because dtype_orig is nulled for DataFrame inputs, this branch is skipped and no warning is issued even when array.dtype (after conversion) differs from the original DataFrame dtype.

3) Missing inference from per-column dtypes: check_array assumes all dtype info lives on array.dtype and does not inspect array.dtypes. Location: sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_array (dtype_orig detection block). Correct relationship to determine dtype_orig for DataFrame-like inputs (per investigation): if getattr(array, "dtypes", None) is present and dtypes.unique() has length 1 then dtype_orig should be set to that unique dtype (e.g., uniques[0]); otherwise dtype_orig should be left as None to avoid spurious warnings for mixed-dtype DataFrames.

4) Scope: check_array is the centralized validation path used by check_X_y and many estimators. Location: repository-wide callers of sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_array. Effect: the incorrect dtype detection in check_array suppresses warnings across all callers that rely on warn_on_dtype.

5) Investigation gap (evidence limitation): the analysis was static and did not include runtime traces or unit tests. Location: agent investigation steps (reported). Effect: while the static code evidence demonstrates the logic error and the intended dtype inference formula (dtypes.unique() length 1 -> use uniques[0]), there is no runtime confirmation or test included in the investigation.
StepsToReproduce: Create a pandas DataFrame with object dtype, e.g. df = pd.DataFrame([[1,2,3],[2,3,4]], dtype=object).
Call from sklearn.utils.validation import check_array and run checked = check_array(df, warn_on_dtype=True).
Observe whether a DataConversionWarning is emitted.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When check_array(..., warn_on_dtype=True) is called with a pandas.DataFrame that is converted to a different numpy dtype (e.g., object -> float64), a DataConversionWarning is emitted stating the input dtype and the converted dtype.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: No DataConversionWarning is emitted when calling check_array(..., warn_on_dtype=True) with a pandas.DataFrame (dtype=object), even though the data is converted to a numpy dtype (e.g., float64).

problem_statement.Title: n_neighbors float not validated: float (e.g. 3.0) reaches Cython and triggers TypeError
problem_statement.Description: Passing a float for n_neighbors (for example NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=3.0) or kneighbors(..., n_neighbors=3.0)) is accepted by the Python API but later used as an array dimension / integer parameter in lower-level code, causing a TypeError from Cython/Numpy instead of a clear parameter validation error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. sklearn/neighbors/base.py::NeighborsBase.__init__ — constructor stores self.n_neighbors = n_neighbors without validation. Wrong assumption: callers always pass integer-valued objects; location encodes acceptance of arbitrary numeric types.

2. sklearn/neighbors/base.py::KNeighborsMixin.kneighbors — method accepts a per-call n_neighbors (or falls back to self.n_neighbors) and uses it in integer-only operations without validation. Wrong assumption: n_neighbors is integer-like. Relevant uses that require integer inputs: arithmetic 'n_neighbors += 1' and calls such as np.argpartition(dist, n_neighbors - 1, axis=1) which require integer k.

3. sklearn/neighbors/base.py::KNeighborsMixin.kneighbors_graph — uses n_neighbors in array sizing/steps assuming integer values. Wrong assumption: n_neighbors is integer. Exact relationships/formulas dependent on integer n_neighbors: n_nonzero = n_samples1 * n_neighbors; A_indptr = np.arange(0, n_nonzero + 1, n_neighbors); A_data = np.ones(n_samples1 * n_neighbors). These expressions require n_neighbors to be an integer to produce correct shapes and indices.

4. sklearn/neighbors/binary_tree.pxi::NeighborsHeap.__init__ — uses the passed n_nbrs directly as a numpy array dimension: e.g., np.zeros((n_pts, n_nbrs), dtype=DTYPE, ...). Wrong assumption: n_nbrs is an integer. When a float (e.g. 3.0) reaches this call, numpy.zeros raises TypeError: "'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer". This is the location where the unhelpful low-level error is raised.

5. Attribute reassignment gap — NeighborsBase exposes self.n_neighbors as a plain attribute with no property setter. Wrong assumption: n_neighbors is only set via constructor or per-call arguments. Evidence: code allows estimator.n_neighbors = 3.0 to bypass any constructor-level checks, reintroducing the float into downstream code paths.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors; from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs; X, y = make_blobs(); nbrs = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=3.0); nbrs.fit(X); nbrs.kneighbors(X)"

Alternatively: python -c "from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors; from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs; X, y = make_blobs(); nbrs = NearestNeighbors(); nbrs.fit(X); nbrs.kneighbors(X, n_neighbors=3.0)"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible: Passing n_neighbors=3.0 should either be accepted (treated as 3) so calls like NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=3.0) and kneighbors(..., n_neighbors=3.0) succeed, while non-integer floats (e.g. 3.5) should raise a clear, early ValueError stating that n_neighbors must be an integer.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When n_neighbors is a float (e.g. 3.0 or 3.5), the code allows the value through to Cython/Numpy which then raises a low-level TypeError: "'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer" from sklearn/neighbors/binary_tree.pxi (NeighborsHeap.__init__) or other downstream calls (np.argpartition, array sizing), producing an unhelpful error message rather than a clear parameter validation error.

@amueller I think there is a lot of other estimators and Python functions in general where dtype isn't explicitely checked and wrong dtype just raises an exception later on.

Take for instance,
```py
import numpy as np

x = np.array([1])
np.sum(x, axis=1.)
```
which produces,
```py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/numpy/core/fromnumeric.py", line 1882, in sum
    out=out, **kwargs)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/numpy/core/_methods.py", line 32, in _sum
    return umr_sum(a, axis, dtype, out, keepdims)
TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer
```
so pretty much the same exception as in the original post, with no indications of what is wrong exactly. Here it's straightforward because we only provided one parameter, but the same is true for more complex constructions. 

So I'm not sure that starting to enforce int/float dtype of parameters, estimator by estimator is a solution here. In general don't think there is a need to do more parameter validation than what is done e.g. in numpy or pandas. If we want to do it, some generic type validation based on annotaitons (e.g. https://github.com/agronholm/typeguard) might be easier but also require more maintenance time and probably harder to implement while Python 2.7 is supported.

problem_statement.Title: Mixture estimators lack clusterer-compatible API (no labels_, no fit_predict, no n_clusters alias)
problem_statement.Description: GaussianMixture and BayesianGaussianMixture use n_components, do not persist training-set labels_ after fit, and do not implement fit_predict, producing an API mismatch with clusterer estimators (e.g., KMeans).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) BaseMixture.fit does not persist training labels (assumption: mixture estimators should not expose labels_). Location: sklearn/mixture/base.py::BaseMixture.fit. Evidence: fit calls self._set_parameters(best_params) and sets n_iter_ but never assigns self.labels_. The labels for the training set can be computed as: self.labels_ = self._estimate_weighted_log_prob(X).argmax(axis=1).

2) BaseMixture provides no fit_predict convenience (assumption: no clusterer-style fit_predict is required). Location: sklearn/mixture/base.py::BaseMixture (no fit_predict method). Evidence: clusterers define fit_predict to return training labels (KMeans.fit_predict returns self.fit(...).labels_) and legacy sklearn/mixture/gmm.py::GMM.fit_predict shows precedent.

3) BaseMixture exposes n_components but not an n_clusters alias (assumption: only n_components is part of the public API). Location: sklearn/mixture/base.py::BaseMixture and subclasses (e.g., sklearn/mixture/gaussian_mixture.py::__init__). Evidence: cluster APIs use n_clusters; BaseMixture._initialize_parameters uses cluster.KMeans(n_clusters=self.n_components, ...), indicating conceptual equivalence and that n_clusters could/should alias n_components.

4) GaussianMixture and BayesianGaussianMixture docstrings omit labels_ from public attributes (assumption: labels_ is not part of the public API). Location: sklearn/mixture/gaussian_mixture.py::GaussianMixture docstring and sklearn/mixture/bayesian_mixture.py::BayesianGaussianMixture docstring. Evidence: class attribute lists include weights_, means_, covariances_, etc., but not labels_.

5) The omission ignores prior precedent (assumption: legacy behavior is irrelevant). Location: sklearn/mixture/gmm.py::GMM.fit_predict (legacy implementation provided fit_predict), showing a precedent for exposing fit_predict on mixture estimators.

6) The investigation did not verify tests or edge cases that could be affected by adding labels_/fit_predict or by adding a constructor-level alias (n_clusters) (assumption: adding these attributes/methods is safe without test changes). Location: test suite and get_params/clone semantics were not inspected (e.g., sklearn/mixture/tests/, estimator cloning logic). Evidence/gap noted by investigator: tests may assume absence of labels_, _estimate_weighted_log_prob(X) shape/availability in edge cases (small n_samples, warm_start) was not exhaustively traced, and constructor aliasing would affect get_params/clone semantics.
StepsToReproduce: Create or load a dataset X (array-like, shape [n_samples, n_features]).
Instantiate a mixture estimator, e.g. GaussianMixture(n_components=2).
Verify presence of training labels: check hasattr(model, 'labels_') or access model.labels_. (Observed: attribute missing / AttributeError.)
Attempt to call model.fit_predict(X). (Observed: AttributeError / method missing.)
Check for clusterer-style parameter name: verify hasattr(model, 'n_clusters') or attempt to read model.n_clusters. (Observed: attribute missing / AttributeError.)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After fit(X), a mixture estimator should expose the training-set component assignments (labels_) and provide the clusterer convenience method fit_predict(X) returning those labels; users should be able to query a clusterer-style name for the number of components (n_clusters) equivalently to n_components.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After fit(X), GaussianMixture and BayesianGaussianMixture do not set labels_, do not implement fit_predict, and expose the parameter only as n_components (no n_clusters alias). Attempts to access labels_, call fit_predict, or read n_clusters raise AttributeError or are unavailable.

problem_statement.Title: OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore') truncates long string categories in numpy fixed-length string arrays, causing ValueError
problem_statement.Description: When OneHotEncoder is used with handle_unknown='ignore' and the input X is a NumPy array with a fixed-length string/bytes dtype, transform can perform in-place replacement of unknown entries with a category string that is longer than the per-element buffer. The replacement is truncated by NumPy and later encoding raises a ValueError about unseen labels.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in _BaseEncoder._check_X (sklearn/preprocessing/_encoders.py::_BaseEncoder._check_X): the code only converts list-like string inputs to object dtype and leaves NumPy fixed-length string/bytes dtypes as-is. The condition used is effectively: if not hasattr(X, 'dtype') and np.issubdtype(X_temp.dtype, np.str_): X = check_array(X, dtype=np.object) else: X = X_temp. This encodes the assumption that keeping fixed-length NumPy string dtypes is safe.

2) Incorrect assumption in _BaseEncoder._transform (sklearn/preprocessing/_encoders.py::_BaseEncoder._transform): the code performs an in-place assignment of the replacement category via Xi[~valid_mask] = self.categories_[i][0]. If len(self.categories_[i][0]) > Xi.dtype.itemsize (i.e. replacement string is longer than the fixed-length buffer of Xi), NumPy truncates the string. The truncated value no longer matches categories_, which leads to downstream errors.

3) Duplicate vulnerable logic in OneHotEncoder._transform_new (sklearn/preprocessing/_encoders.py::OneHotEncoder._transform_new): the same dtype-detection/conversion branch appears here, so transform can remain vulnerable even if _check_X is changed. This duplicates the incorrect assumption that NumPy fixed-length string dtypes need not be converted.

4) Symptom raising in label encoding helpers (sklearn/preprocessing/label.py::_encode_numpy): _encode_numpy assumes values match uniques exactly and raises ValueError when differences exist. The relationship is: diff = _encode_check_unknown(values, uniques); if diff: raise ValueError(...). Truncation produces altered values (e.g. '111111' from '11111111'), so diff is non-empty and a ValueError is raised. This is a symptom, not the root cause.

5) Shared impact on sibling encoders: OrdinalEncoder and any code paths that rely on the same _check_X/_transform logic inherit the same incorrect assumptions and are therefore affected (location: sklearn/preprocessing/_encoders.py::OrdinalEncoder).
StepsToReproduce: Create NumPy test array: test = np.array(['55555','22']).reshape((-1,1))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Transform completes without raising an exception when handle_unknown='ignore'. For the repro arrays, transform returns a 2x4 sparse matrix with all False except the known '22' encoded at the appropriate row/column (no ValueError).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Transform raises ValueError: "y contains previously unseen labels: ['111111']" because a long replacement category string was truncated when assigned into a NumPy fixed-length string array, producing values that do not match the learned categories.

problem_statement.Title: GaussianMixture.fit_predict returns labels computed from wrong initialization when n_init > 1
problem_statement.Description: When GaussianMixture is fitted with n_init > 1, fit_predict(X) can return labels computed from the parameters of the last initialization attempt rather than from the best parameters stored on the model. As a result, fit_predict(X) and predict(X) can disagree.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Ordering bug in sklearn/mixture/base.py :: BaseMixture.fit_predict — the method performs a final E-step before restoring the best parameters. Concretely, the code computes "_, log_resp = self._e_step(X)" and later calls "self._set_parameters(best_params)" and then returns "log_resp.argmax(axis=1)". This assumes the final E-step used the same parameters that will be stored, which is incorrect when multiple initializations are used.

2) Labels returned by fit_predict are computed as labels = log_resp.argmax(axis=1), where log_resp is the second value returned by self._e_step(X). Because log_resp is computed before _set_parameters(best_params), these labels reflect the last initialization's derived attributes, not the best parameters that are stored on the object. (Location: sklearn/mixture/base.py, BaseMixture.fit_predict)

3) Predict uses the model's stored parameters to compute labels (i.e., predict relies on attributes populated by _set_parameters). Therefore, predict(X) and the labels returned by fit_predict(X) can differ when fit_predict computed log_resp from a different parameter set. (Location: sklearn/mixture/base.py, BaseMixture.predict and BaseMixture._e_step)

4) Subclass _set_parameters implementations (e.g., sklearn/mixture/gaussian_mixture.py :: GaussianMixture._set_parameters and sklearn/mixture/bayesian_mixture.py :: BayesianGaussianMixture._set_parameters) populate derived attributes (weights_, means_, covariances_, precisions_, precisions_cholesky_, weight_concentration_, mean_precision_, etc.) that _e_step/_estimate_log_prob_resp use. These relationships mean that computing responsibilities (_e_step) before calling _set_parameters leads to responsibilities that do not correspond to the stored parameters. (Locations: sklearn/mixture/gaussian_mixture.py, sklearn/mixture/bayesian_mixture.py)

5) Missing test coverage allowed the regression: tests/mixture/test_gmm.py (and related tests for BayesianGaussianMixture and warm_start paths) do not assert that GaussianMixture(n_components=K, n_init>1).fit_predict(X) == model.predict(X), so the inconsistency was not caught by the test suite. (Location: test suite; evidence: original reproducer shows assert_array_equal(c1, c2) fails when n_init=5)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For the same input X and model configuration, fit_predict(X) should return the same labels as predict(X) after fitting the model, regardless of n_init.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When n_init > 1, fit_predict(X) frequently returns a label array that differs from predict(X). An assertion comparing the two label arrays (e.g., assert_array_equal) fails (high mismatch rate observed in reproducer).

problem_statement.Title: KernelPCA returns components with nondeterministic sign (per-run sign flips)
problem_statement.Description: When running KernelPCA (rbf kernel) multiple times on the same data, the returned components (projections) are identical in magnitude but some component columns have inverted signs between runs.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) KernelPCA leaves eigenvector signs undetermined: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : KernelPCA._fit_transform computes and stores eigenvectors in self.alphas_ (shape (n_samples, n_components)) and eigenvalues in self.lambdas_ (shape (n_components,)) but does not apply any deterministic sign canonicalization. The code assumes eigenvector sign is irrelevant and therefore allows ±v ambiguity to persist.

2) Downstream outputs depend directly on self.alphas_: fit_transform returns self.alphas_ * np.sqrt(self.lambdas_) and transform returns np.dot(K, self.alphas_ / np.sqrt(self.lambdas_)); because these formulas use self.alphas_ directly, any arbitrary sign choice in self.alphas_ produces corresponding sign flips in user-visible outputs. Locations: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : fit_transform, transform, fit, _fit_inverse_transform.

3) Existing codebase convention is to canonicalize component signs (svd_flip) but KernelPCA does not follow it: sklearn/decomposition/pca.py uses sklearn.utils.extmath.svd_flip to make the largest-absolute-entry in each component positive; KernelPCA lacks an svd_flip (or equivalent) call, creating inconsistency with other decompositions. Location: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py (absence of svd_flip), related helper: sklearn/utils/extmath.py:svd_flip.

4) The implementation therefore encodes the incorrect assumption that no additional canonicalization is required after eigen-decomposition; this assumption is encoded in KernelPCA._fit_transform and propagates to all methods that read self.alphas_.

Note: the investigation also identified gaps (no numeric trace demonstrating transform/inverse_transform equivalence post-canonicalization, and unresolved nondeterminism when eigenvalues are equal) but these are test/coverage gaps rather than additional root-cause items.
StepsToReproduce: Import KernelPCA and load your dataset X.
Instantiate pca = KernelPCA(n_components=7, kernel='rbf', copy_X=False, n_jobs=-1).
Call pca.fit_transform(X) and save the result (run A).
Repeat pca.fit_transform(X) in a new Python process or after re-instantiating pca and save the result (run B).
Compare run A and run B: observe that corresponding component columns are identical in magnitude but some columns are multiplied by -1.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Repeated runs of KernelPCA with the same input data and equivalent environment produce the same projected components (no unexpected per-component sign flips).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Between runs on the same dataset, KernelPCA returns projections that match in magnitude but some component columns have inverted signs (e.g. one run has [-0.44457617 -0.18155886 ... 0.18124364], another run has [0.44457617 0.18155886 ... -0.18124364]).

problem_statement.Title: len(Pipeline) and slicing with len() raise TypeError because _BaseComposition lacks __len__
problem_statement.Description: Calling len(pipe) or using pipe[:len(pipe)] raises a TypeError because Pipeline (and other composition subclasses) do not implement Python's sizing protocol (__len__). The codebase provides __getitem__/slicing on Pipeline but no length implementation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _BaseComposition missing __len__ (sklearn/utils/metaestimators.py): the base composition class does not implement __len__; the codebase assumes composition objects need not provide Python's sizing protocol. Location: sklearn/utils/metaestimators.py:_BaseComposition.

2) Pipeline defines __getitem__ but not __len__ (sklearn/pipeline.py): Pipeline implements slicing/__getitem__ and uses self.steps (class attribute Pipeline._required_parameters = ['steps']) but does not implement __len__, so calling len(Pipeline) raises TypeError. Location: sklearn/pipeline.py:Pipeline.

3) Subclass-required-attribute mapping is the intended source for length (multiple files): subclasses expose which attribute holds the estimator list via the class attribute _required_parameters; examples: Pipeline._required_parameters = ['steps'], FeatureUnion._required_parameters = ['transformer_list'], ColumnTransformer._required_parameters = ['transformers'], VotingClassifier._required_parameters = ['estimators']. The correct length relationship is: iterate over getattr(self, '_required_parameters', []), for each attr do items = getattr(self, attr, None); if items is not None return len(items); otherwise raise TypeError("object of type '{}' has no len()".format(type(self).__name__)). This relationship (len should be derived from the first non-None required-parameter attribute) is encoded by the subclasses' _required_parameters values. Relevant locations: sklearn/pipeline.py (Pipeline, FeatureUnion), sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py (ColumnTransformer), sklearn/ensemble/voting_classifier.py (VotingClassifier).

4) Implicit assumption that the required-attribute is always present and sequence-like: code assumes attributes named in _required_parameters are populated with a sequence at construction time; there is no handling for missing/None/non-sequence values. This assumption is encoded across _BaseComposition and the subclass constructors (sklearn/utils/metaestimators.py and the subclass files listed above) and is a gap that affects behavior in edge cases (e.g. attribute absent, None, or containing placeholders like 'passthrough').
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sklearn import svm; from sklearn.datasets import samples_generator; from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_regression; from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline; X,y = samples_generator.make_classification(n_informative=5, n_redundant=0, random_state=42); anova_filter = SelectKBest(f_regression, k=5); clf = svm.SVC(kernel='linear'); pipe = Pipeline([('anova', anova_filter), ('svc', clf)]); print(len(pipe))"
Or in an interactive session: create the Pipeline as above and evaluate len(pipe) or pipe[:len(pipe)].
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: len(pipe) returns the number of steps in the Pipeline (an integer); slicing pipe[:len(pipe)] returns a Pipeline equivalent to the original (no exception). Sizing (len()) should be supported consistently for Pipeline and other composition estimators that expose lists via _required_parameters.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling len(pipe) raises TypeError (object of type 'Pipeline' has no len()), and expressions that use len(pipe) such as pipe[:len(pipe)] raise the same error.

problem_statement.Title: IsolationForest.__init__ does not expose warm_start (API/documentation omission)
problem_statement.Description: IsolationForest inherits warm_start behavior from BaseBagging but its public constructor does not accept or document the warm_start parameter. As a result users cannot pass warm_start at construction time and must set the attribute manually after instantiation to reuse/extend a fitted forest.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Missing constructor parameter (sklearn/ensemble/iforest.py:IsolationForest.__init__): the __init__ signature does not include warm_start and the super().__init__ call does not forward warm_start, encoding the incorrect assumption that warm_start should not be a user-facing parameter for IsolationForest.

2. Base class implements required behavior (sklearn/ensemble/bagging.py:BaseBagging._fit / BaseBagging.__init__): BaseBagging defines self.warm_start and BaseBagging._fit implements incremental addition logic using self.warm_start. Specifically, BaseBagging._fit computes n_more_estimators = self.n_estimators - len(self.estimators_) and uses self.warm_start to decide whether to reuse existing estimators or reinitialize. The omission in IsolationForest.__init__ prevents users from setting warm_start at construction despite this existing behavior.

3. problem_statement.Title: Unsafe comparison to 'auto' in _estimate_mi causes errors when discrete_features is a NumPy array
problem_statement.Description: In sklearn/feature_selection/mutual_info_.py::_estimate_mi the code uses the expression "if discrete_features == 'auto':". If callers pass discrete_features as a NumPy array (index array or boolean mask) that equality yields an ndarray of booleans and the if statement triggers an ambiguous-truth/value error (now or in future NumPy versions). Tests do not cover NumPy-array inputs for discrete_features.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Unsafe scalar/string equality assumption in sklearn/feature_selection/mutual_info_.py :: _estimate_mi: the code performs "if discrete_features == 'auto': discrete_features = issparse(X)" which assumes discrete_features is scalar-like. When discrete_features is a numpy.ndarray the comparison produces an ndarray of booleans and makes the if statement invalid (ambiguous truth-value).

2. Existing array-handling logic is correct but is bypassed by the prior equality check in sklearn/feature_selection/mutual_info_.py :: _estimate_mi: later code expects to operate on an array-like value via
    discrete_features = np.asarray(discrete_features)
    if discrete_features.dtype != 'bool':
        discrete_mask[discrete_features] = True
    else:
        discrete_mask = discrete_features
   This relationship shows array inputs (index arrays vs boolean masks) should be handled by dtype-based branching, but the unsafe '== "auto"' check prevents that path for ndarray inputs.

3. Call-site forwarding: mutual_info_regression and mutual_info_classif in sklearn/feature_selection/mutual_info_.py simply forward the discrete_features argument to _estimate_mi, so the incorrect assumption is encoded only in _estimate_mi (callers do not adapt inputs).

4. Test coverage gap in sklearn/feature_selection/tests/test_mutual_info.py: tests exercise Python-list indices (e.g. [2]) but do not test numpy.ndarray index inputs (np.array([2])) nor numpy boolean masks (np.array([...], dtype=bool)), allowing the bug to go undetected.

5. Potential similar issues elsewhere: other files contain unchecked "== 'auto'" comparisons (examples found in sklearn/cluster/hierarchical.py, sklearn/cluster/k_means_.py, sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py). These sites were discovered by search but not audited to confirm whether their parameters can be array-like and therefore vulnerable to the same ambiguous-equality issue.
StepsToReproduce: Create a feature matrix X and target y (e.g. random or simple deterministic data).
Call mutual_info_classif(X, y, discrete_features=np.array([2]), random_state=0) and observe the error.
Call mutual_info_classif(X, y, discrete_features=np.array([False, False, True]), random_state=0) and observe the error.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: mutual_info_classif / mutual_info_regression accept discrete_features passed as list, numpy.ndarray of indices, or numpy boolean mask and produce the same result for equivalent inputs (e.g. list [2], np.array([2]), and mask [False, False, True] should be handled consistently) without raising ambiguous-truth or type errors.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When discrete_features is a numpy.ndarray (index array or boolean mask), the expression "discrete_features == 'auto'" yields an array of booleans; the subsequent if statement triggers an ambiguous truth-value error (ValueError) — preventing ndarray inputs from being accepted. Tests do not include these ndarray cases.

problem_statement.Title: Estimator __repr__ with set_config(print_changed_only=True) raises ValueError for array-like parameters
problem_statement.Description: When pretty-printing an estimator with set_config(print_changed_only=True), repr() raises ValueError: "The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous" if one of the estimator's init parameters is array-like (e.g. Cs=np.array([0.1, 1])). The failure occurs inside the pretty-printing utilities.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in sklearn/utils/_pprint.py:_changed_params: it uses the expression "v != init_params[k]" which assumes the comparison yields a single boolean scalar. This is encoded in sklearn/utils/_pprint.py:_changed_params and causes a ValueError when v is a numpy.ndarray because (v != init_params[k]) is an array of booleans, not a single truth value.

2) The current _changed_params condition pairs the above comparison with a NaN special-case: "(v != init_params[k] and not (is_scalar_nan(init_params[k]) and is_scalar_nan(v)))". The incorrect assumption is that "v != init_params[k]" is a scalar boolean; the correct relationship needed to determine whether a parameter changed is: equal := (is_scalar_nan(init) and is_scalar_nan(v)) OR array-aware equality between v and init (e.g. np.array_equal(np.asarray(v), np.asarray(init))); then a parameter is changed if NOT equal. This equality definition must be used where the current scalar != is used.

3) Both pretty-print code paths call the same buggy function and therefore both are affected: sklearn/utils/_pprint.py:_EstimatorPrettyPrinter._pprint_estimator and sklearn/utils/_pprint.py:_safe_repr delegate to _changed_params, so the scalar-comparison assumption in _changed_params impacts single-line and multi-line repr paths.

4) A shape/type mismatch in an estimator exposes the bug: sklearn/linear_model/logistic.py:LogisticRegressionCV.__init__ defines the default Cs=10 (scalar), but a user-supplied Cs=np.array([0.1, 1]) produces an ndarray vs scalar comparison in _changed_params. The assumption that constructor defaults and supplied values are directly comparable with "!=" is therefore unsafe for array-like parameters.

5) Test coverage gap: sklearn/utils/tests/test_pprint.py does not include cases where constructor parameters are array-like (e.g. numpy.ndarray) while print_changed_only=True, so the scalar-only comparison bug was not caught by tests. This missing coverage is encoded in sklearn/utils/tests/test_pprint.py.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import sklearn; import numpy as np; from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV; sklearn.set_config(print_changed_only=True); print(LogisticRegressionCV(Cs=np.array([0.1, 1])))"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Printing the estimator with print_changed_only=True should succeed and display only the parameters that differ from their defaults (in user-visible form). It must not raise a ValueError when a parameter is array-like.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling repr/print on the estimator raises ValueError: "The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()" originating from the boolean test in sklearn/utils/_pprint.py when comparing an array-like parameter to its default.

problem_statement.Title: Voting.fit raises AttributeError when an estimator is set to None and sample_weight is provided
problem_statement.Description: When a VotingClassifier/Regressor has an estimator slot set to None (via set_params(name=None)), calling fit(..., sample_weight=...) triggers an AttributeError because the sample_weight support validation calls has_fit_parameter on None.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) _BaseVoting.fit (sklearn/ensemble/voting.py): Assumes every entry in self.estimators is an estimator object and calls has_fit_parameter(step, 'sample_weight') without checking if step is None. 2) has_fit_parameter (sklearn/utils/validation.py): Assumes callers pass a real estimator with a .fit method; inspecting signature(estimator.fit) raises AttributeError if estimator is None. 3) _BaseVoting.fit (sklearn/ensemble/voting.py) inconsistency: Later fitting code intentionally skips None estimators (e.g., building self.estimators_ and passing only non-None clfs to _parallel_fit_estimator), so the upfront sample_weight validation should likewise skip None but does not. 4) _parallel_fit_estimator (sklearn/ensemble/voting.py): Assumes estimator is non-None when calling estimator.fit(...); the validation step must preserve the same invariant (i.e., only validate real estimators). 5) _BaseVoting._weights_not_none (sklearn/ensemble/voting.py): Encodes the class's intended behavior that None entries represent absent estimators by filtering weights for non-None estimators, demonstrating that None is an expected placeholder but the sample_weight validation does not honor that.

problem_statement.Title: IndexError in LogisticRegressionCV.fit (refit=False) due to wrong multi_class variable reference
problem_statement.Description: When fitting LogisticRegressionCV with refit=False, an IndexError 'too many indices for array' is raised during aggregation of coefficient paths. The failure occurs inside LogisticRegressionCV.fit while averaging coefs_paths after cross-validation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Wrong variable reference selecting aggregation branch (sklearn/linear_model/logistic.py : LogisticRegressionCV.fit): the code checks self.multi_class instead of the resolved local variable multi_class (which is set by multi_class = _check_multi_class(self.multi_class, ...)). This causes the branch chosen for averaging coefs_paths to mismatch how coefs_paths was constructed.

2. Inconsistent shape assumption between producer and consumer of coefs_paths (sklearn/linear_model/logistic.py : LogisticRegressionCV.fit and helpers _log_reg_scoring_path / _logistic_regression_path): the helper calls are invoked with the local multi_class, so coefs_paths shapes depend on the resolved multi_class. The aggregator erroneously uses self.multi_class and therefore may index coefs_paths with the wrong axis ordering.

3. Exact indexing/aggregation relationship encoded in the method (sklearn/linear_model/logistic.py : LogisticRegressionCV.fit): best_indices = np.argmax(scores, axis=1) and the code uses two indexing patterns to compute w = np.mean([...], axis=0):
   - For OvR (intended): w = np.mean([coefs_paths[i, best_indices[i], :] for i in range(len(folds))], axis=0)
   - For multinomial (intended): w = np.mean([coefs_paths[:, i, best_indices[i], :] for i in range(len(folds))], axis=0)
   Because the wrong branch can be taken, the chosen indexing pattern can have too many indices for the actual coefs_paths array, producing the observed IndexError.

4. Single incorrect assumption encoded at one location (sklearn/linear_model/logistic.py : LogisticRegressionCV.fit): the method assumes the instance attribute self.multi_class already reflects the resolved multi_class value; this single incorrect reference is the proximate cause of the shape/indexing mismatch.

problem_statement.Title: GridSearch fails for NCA because check_scalar's isinstance-based type check rejects NumPy scalar parameters
problem_statement.Description: When running GridSearchCV with parameter grids that contain NumPy scalar values (e.g., np.int64, np.float64), NeighborhoodComponentsAnalysis and other estimators that use sklearn.utils.validation.check_scalar raise TypeError. The validation helper currently requires exact Python built-in types and therefore treats NumPy scalars as invalid.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Overly strict type assertion in sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_scalar — check_scalar enforces the requirement via an isinstance check (effectively: if not isinstance(x, target_type): raise TypeError), which rejects NumPy scalar types (np.int64, np.float64, np.bool_) even though they are numeric equivalents of Python builtins. Location: sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_scalar.

2) NCA uses check_scalar with built-in types and therefore inherits the rejection of NumPy scalars. Example calls in NeighborhoodComponentsAnalysis._validate_params: check_scalar(self.n_components, 'n_components', int, 1) and check_scalar(self.tol, 'tol', float, 0.). Location: sklearn/neighbors/nca.py::_validate_params.

3) ParameterGrid/GridSearchCV produces and assigns parameter values as-provided (e.g., elements from np.arange are np.int64) and BaseEstimator.set_params does not coerce them, so NumPy scalars reach estimator validators unchanged. Location: sklearn/model_selection/_search.py::ParameterGrid and BaseEstimator.set_params (assignment path).

4) Test-suite expectations currently encode strict builtin-type behavior for some cases (tests in sklearn/utils/tests/test_validation.py assert that a Python int is invalid where float is required). Any behavioral change to check_scalar must preserve intended strictness where explicitly tested (e.g., rejection of Python int when target_type is float). Location: sklearn/utils/tests/test_validation.py::test_check_scalar_invalid.

5) Some estimators may use inline isinstance checks (not check_scalar); those code paths also encode the same incorrect assumption and would not be addressed by changing check_scalar alone. Location: various estimators that perform manual isinstance(x, int/float/bool) checks (repo-wide).
StepsToReproduce: Create random data X (shape (100, 10)) and labels y (length 100).
Create a Pipeline with ('nca', NeighborhoodComponentsAnalysis()) and ('knn', KNeighborsClassifier()).
Define params = {'nca__tol': [0.1, 0.5, 1], 'nca__n_components': np.arange(1, 10)} (note: contains Python int 1 and np.int64 from np.arange).
Instantiate GridSearchCV(estimator=pipe, param_grid=params, error_score='raise') and call .fit(X, y).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: GridSearchCV should accept parameter values produced from NumPy constructs (e.g., np.arange, numpy scalar literals) and complete the search without raising TypeError from estimator parameter validation. Estimators should accept numeric NumPy scalar equivalents of the expected Python builtin types in user-provided parameter grids (so numeric scalar parameter values do not cause a validation error).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: GridSearchCV.fit raises TypeError during estimator parameter validation (for example, complaining that 'tol' must be an instance of float but got np.int64, or that 'n_components' must be an instance of int but got np.int64), causing the grid search to fail.

> Edit: I am currently trying out another idea. I'll update this issue when it becomes something presentable.

maybe you can pitch me and if you want I can give a hand :)
I would have loved to using the typing system to get this to work:

```py
def __init__(

```

but that would have to wait for [PEP 593](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0593/). In the end, I would want the validator to be a part of sklearn estimators. Using typing (as above) is a natural choice, since it keeps the parameter and its constraint physically close to each other.

If we can't use typing, these constraints can be place in a `_validate_parameters` method. This will be called at the beginning of fit to do parameter validation. Estimators that need more validation will overwrite the method, call `super()._validate_parameters` and do more validation. For example, `LogesticRegression`'s `penalty='l2'` only works for specify solvers. `skconfig` defines a framework for handling these situations, but I think it would be too hard to learn.

> If we can't use typing, these constraints can be place in a _validate_parameters method. 

This is already the case for a subset of the estimators (`_check_params` or `_validate_input`). But it's often incomplete.

> skconfig defines a framework for handling these situations, but I think it would be too hard to learn.

Your framework does way more than what I proposed. Maybe we can do this in 2 steps:
First, a simple single param check which only checks its type and if its value is acceptable in general (e.g. positive for a number of clusters). This will raise a standard error message
Then a more advanced check, depending on the data (e.g. number of clusters should be < n_samples) or consistency across params (e.g. solver + penalty). These checks require more elaborate error messages.

problem_statement.Title: ZeroDivisionError in BaseLibSVM._sparse_fit when support_vectors_ is empty for sparse input
problem_statement.Description: Fitting an SVM on sparse input can raise a ZeroDivisionError in BaseLibSVM._sparse_fit when the trained model has zero support vectors or when classes_ has length 1. The error occurs while building a CSR representation for dual_coef_.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in sklearn/svm/base.py:BaseLibSVM._sparse_fit that if hasattr(self, "classes_") then len(self.classes_) >= 2. The code computes n_class = len(self.classes_) - 1, which yields n_class == 0 when len(self.classes_) == 1; this allows a division by zero in downstream expressions. (Exact expression: n_class = len(self.classes_) - 1)

2) Incorrect assumption in sklearn/svm/base.py:BaseLibSVM._sparse_fit that there is at least one support vector (n_SV > 0). When n_SV == 0 dual_coef_indices.size == 0 and constructing indptr with a step derived from dual_coef_indices.size becomes invalid. (Relevant expression: dual_coef_indptr = np.arange(0, dual_coef_indices.size + 1, dual_coef_indices.size / n_class))

3) Wrong numeric operation used for CSR indptr step in sklearn/svm/base.py:BaseLibSVM._sparse_fit: the code uses floating division (dual_coef_indices.size / n_class) to compute the arange step for indptr, producing a non-integer step or a float 0/0. The indptr step must be an integer count. (Correct relationship to preserve: step = dual_coef_indices.size // n_class; dual_coef_indptr = np.arange(0, dual_coef_indices.size + 1, step))

4) libsvm_sparse return shapes are already compatible with empty outputs (sklearn/svm/libsvm_sparse.pyx). sv_coef_data length = (n_class - 1) * SV_len and SV_indptr length = SV_len + 1 can legitimately be zero-length when SV_len == 0 or n_class == 1; therefore the crash is caused by Python-side CSR assembly not handling these zero-length arrays, not by the Cython trainer.

5) Missing handling of the empty dual-coefficient case in sklearn/svm/base.py:BaseLibSVM._sparse_fit. The safe representation for "no dual coefficients" is a CSR matrix of shape (n_class, 0) (e.g., sp.csr_matrix((n_class, 0))). The code does not create this representation when n_SV == 0 or when n_class == 0, leading to the ZeroDivisionError described above.
StepsToReproduce: Create dense training arrays x_train and y_train as in the original report (4 samples, 4 features; y_train = [0.04, 0.04, 0.10, 0.16]).
Instantiate an SVR with kernel='linear' and the same parameters shown in the report.
Convert x_train to sparse: xtrain = scipy.sparse.csr_matrix(x_train).
Call model.fit(xtrain, y_train) — this raises the ZeroDivisionError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Fitting the estimator on the sparse input completes without raising an exception. The estimator ends up in a valid state for degenerate cases (no support vectors), and attributes that expose dual coefficients reflect an empty result (no dual coefficients). Prediction/decision methods remain callable.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling fit(...) on the sparse input raises a ZeroDivisionError: float division by zero originating from sklearn/svm/base.py where dual_coef_indptr is constructed using dual_coef_indices.size / n_class.

problem_statement.Title: repr(RepeatedKFold) and repr(RepeatedStratifiedKFold) return object.__repr__ instead of parameterized string
problem_statement.Description: Calling repr() on RepeatedKFold() and RepeatedStratifiedKFold() returns the default object representation (memory address) instead of a readable parameterized string like RepeatedKFold(n_splits=5, n_repeats=10, random_state=None).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _RepeatedSplits missing BaseCrossValidator parent (sklearn/model_selection/_split.py — class _RepeatedSplits header): wrong assumption that repeated-wrappers need not inherit BaseCrossValidator. Because _RepeatedSplits does not inherit BaseCrossValidator, RepeatedKFold/RepeatedStratifiedKFold do not get BaseCrossValidator.__repr__ and therefore use object.__repr__.

2) RepeatedKFold.__init__ does not expose n_splits as an instance attribute (sklearn/model_selection/_split.py — RepeatedKFold.__init__): wrong assumption that passing n_splits into cvargs is sufficient for representation. The constructor passes n_splits only via cvargs and does not set self.n_splits, so a repr-builder that inspects instance attributes would not find n_splits.

3) RepeatedStratifiedKFold.__init__ does not expose n_splits as an instance attribute (sklearn/model_selection/_split.py — RepeatedStratifiedKFold.__init__): same wrong assumption and symptom as item 2 for the stratified variant.

4) Representation building relationship (sklearn/model_selection/_split.py — BaseCrossValidator.__repr__ and _build_repr): BaseCrossValidator.__repr__ delegates to _build_repr(self), and _build_repr constructs the textual repr by iterating over the class __init__ signature and using getattr(self, <param_name>, None) for each constructor parameter. Therefore the instance must expose attributes named after the __init__ parameters (e.g., n_splits, n_repeats, random_state) for the readable repr to include them. Evidence: _RepeatedSplits sets self.n_repeats and self.random_state but not self.n_splits, so even if a __repr__ were present, n_splits would be reported as None.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: repr(RepeatedKFold()) and repr(RepeatedStratifiedKFold()) return a human-readable string listing constructor parameters and their values, for example: 'RepeatedKFold(n_splits=5, n_repeats=10, random_state=None)' and 'RepeatedStratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, n_repeats=10, random_state=None)'.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: repr(RepeatedKFold()) and repr(RepeatedStratifiedKFold()) return the default object representation, e.g. '<sklearn.model_selection._split.RepeatedKFold object at 0x0000016421AA4288>' and '<sklearn.model_selection._split.RepeatedStratifiedKFold object at 0x0000016420E115C8>'.

problem_statement.Title: AffinityPropagation returns valid-looking centers/labels when algorithm did not converge
problem_statement.Description: When affinity_propagation reaches the maximum number of iterations without meeting its convergence criterion, the function can return cluster center indices and labels computed from the last iteration instead of reporting a non-converged result. This causes callers to receive a plausible clustering (non -1 labels and a non-empty center array) even though the algorithm did not converge.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Last-iteration exemplar indicator E is treated as a valid final result when the main loop exhausts max_iter — encoded in sklearn/cluster/_affinity_propagation.py:affinity_propagation. Evidence/formula: E = (np.diag(A) + np.diag(R)) > 0; e[:, it % convergence_iter] = E; after the loop I = np.flatnonzero(E); K = I.size. Because E is not reset when the for loop runs to completion without break, a non-converged run where the final E contains True values yields K>0 and the function returns centers/labels.

2) A dead/wrong convergence condition is present in the loop — encoded in sklearn/cluster/_affinity_propagation.py:affinity_propagation. Evidence/formula: the break condition is if (not unconverged and (K > 0)) or (it == max_iter). Iteration uses for it in range(max_iter) (it ranges 0..max_iter-1), so (it == max_iter) is never true; this encodes a logical bug that attempts to special-case loop exhaustion inside the loop instead of handling it correctly after the loop.

3) The for-else branch only prints "Did not converge" but does not enforce the documented non-converged outputs — encoded in sklearn/cluster/_affinity_propagation.py:affinity_propagation. Evidence: the else clause only verbose-prints; it does not modify E/K, so downstream code uses the last E to compute centers/labels despite non-convergence.

4) Return-type inconsistency for the no-exemplar case: when K == 0 the function sets labels = np.array([-1] * n_samples) but cluster_centers_indices = [] (a Python list) instead of a numpy array — encoded in sklearn/cluster/_affinity_propagation.py:affinity_propagation. Evidence: the code issues a ConvergenceWarning and then sets cluster_centers_indices = []. This violates the documented "empty array" return and creates inconsistent return types across branches.

5) Downstream behavior/documentation mismatch: AffinityPropagation.fit and predict rely on the outputs of affinity_propagation and assume empty numpy arrays/labels == -1 indicate non-convergence; however, predict currently raises ValueError when affinity='precomputed', which is a separate documentation/behavior mismatch referenced by sklearn/cluster/_affinity_propagation.py:AffinityPropagation.predict and class docstrings. The investigation noted this as a related inconsistency (not the primary root cause of the wrong outputs).
StepsToReproduce: Fit with a low max_iter so the algorithm does not converge: af = AffinityPropagation(affinity='euclidean', verbose=True, copy=False, max_iter=2).fit(data)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When the affinity propagation algorithm does not converge, the public results should indicate non-convergence in user-visible terms: cluster_centers_indices should be an empty array and labels should be -1 for every sample (and the types should be consistent with other return branches). No misleading non-empty center indices or normal-looking labels should be returned for a non-converged run.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On loop exhaustion without convergence, the function can return a non-empty cluster_centers_indices (e.g. [2]) and normal labels (e.g. [0, 0, 0]) computed from the final iteration's exemplar indicator E, despite printing 'Did not converge' when verbose is enabled. In the K == 0 branch the code returns labels == -1 but uses a Python list for cluster_centers_indices instead of an empty numpy array.

problem_statement.Title: Clustering metrics raise ValueError for object-dtype string labels due to numeric coercion in validation
problem_statement.Description: Passing object-dtype label arrays (e.g. arrays of Python strings) to clustering metrics such as mutual_info_score raises ValueError: "could not convert string to float: 'b'". The failure occurs during input validation before metric computation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. check_clusterings assumes label arrays should be numeric and calls sklearn.utils.validation.check_array without specifying dtype. Location: sklearn/metrics/cluster/_supervised.py: check_clusterings. This lets check_array use its default dtype="numeric".

2. check_array's default behavior coerces object-dtype inputs to np.float64: when dtype == "numeric" and array.dtype.kind == 'O', check_array sets dtype = np.float64 and performs array = array.astype(np.float64), which raises ValueError for string/object data. Location: sklearn/utils/validation.py: check_array. (Logical relationship: dtype == "numeric" && array.dtype.kind == 'O' → dtype = np.float64 → array.astype(np.float64)).

3. The assumption that labels must be numeric is incorrect for clustering metrics: downstream code uses np.unique(..., return_inverse=True) to map arbitrary hashable labels to integer indices and does not require the original label values to be numeric. Location: sklearn/metrics/cluster/_supervised.py and callers such as mutual_info_score, adjusted_rand_score, contingency matrix, entropy, etc. (Relationship: labels → np.unique(labels, return_inverse=True) → integer index array used for contingency/count computations).
StepsToReproduce:
Run the following Python snippet (object-dtype labels) and observe the exception:

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.cluster import mutual_info_score
x = np.random.choice(['a', 'b'], size=20).astype(object)
mutual_info_score(x, x)

This raises: ValueError: could not convert string to float: 'b'.

Run the following Python snippet (flexible/unicode dtype) and observe it currently works (may emit a warning):

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.cluster import mutual_info_score
x = np.random.choice(['a', 'b'], size=20)
mutual_info_score(x, x)

This returns a score (previously worked without warning in older releases).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Clustering metrics (mutual_info_score, adjusted_rand_score, entropy, etc.) accept label arrays containing strings or other object-dtype values and compute the metric normally (at most a warning about non-numeric dtypes), without raising a ValueError during input validation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Providing an object-dtype label array of strings to mutual_info_score (and other clustering metrics that call check_clusterings) raises ValueError: "could not convert string to float: 'b'" because validation coerces the object array to np.float64.

problem_statement.Title: CalibratedClassifierCV fails with set_config(transform_output="pandas") when method='isotonic'
problem_statement.Description: When global set_config(transform_output="pandas") is enabled, CalibratedClassifierCV(method='isotonic') crashes in _CalibratedClassifier.predict_proba because the calibrator's predict/transform returns a pandas container with shape (n_samples, 1) instead of a 1D numpy array, causing a broadcasting error during assignment into a 1D slice of the probabilities array.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect consumer-side assumption in sklearn/calibration.py -> class _CalibratedClassifier -> method predict_proba: the code assumes calibrator.predict(this_pred) returns a 1D numpy ndarray suitable for assignment into proba[:, class_idx]. Location: sklearn/calibration.py (expression: proba[:, class_idx] = calibrator.predict(this_pred)). The correct relationship required by the consumer is that the calibrator output be a 1D array of length n_samples (i.e. shape (n_samples,)), so it can be assigned to proba[:, class_idx].

2) Producer-side behavior that violates the consumer assumption: sklearn/isotonic.py -> IsotonicRegression.predict returns self.transform(T), and transform is wrapped by sklearn/utils/_set_output.py when set_config(transform_output="pandas") is set. As a result, calibrator.predict can return pandas objects (DataFrame shape (n_samples, 1) or Series), not a 1D numpy ndarray. Location: sklearn/isotonic.py:IsotonicRegression.predict and sklearn/utils/_set_output.py (wrapper for transform).

3) Required material relationship for safe assignment (consumer expectation preserved as a formula): the calibrator output must be coerced to a flat 1D array of length n_samples before assigning into proba[:, class_idx]. Concretely, the relationship is: pred = np.asarray(calibrator.predict(this_pred)); pred = pred.ravel(); require pred.shape[0] == proba.shape[0]; then proba[:, class_idx] = pred. This preserves the invariant that the right-hand side is shape (n_samples,) matching proba[:, class_idx].

4) Test coverage gap: there is no existing unit test that exercises CalibratedClassifierCV(method='isotonic') together with set_config(transform_output="pandas"). Location: sklearn/tests/test_calibration.py — absence of such a test allows this regression to pass unnoticed.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling fit/predict/predict_proba on CalibratedClassifierCV(method='isotonic') should not raise an exception when set_config(transform_output="pandas") is enabled. predict_proba and predict should return probability/prediction results with correct shapes (n_samples, n_classes) / (n_samples,) respectively.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A ValueError is raised during predict/predict_proba: "could not broadcast input array from shape (n_samples,1) into shape (n_samples,)" originating from sklearn/calibration.py where the code performs proba[:, class_idx] = calibrator.predict(this_pred). The calibrator (IsotonicRegression) returned a pandas object with shape (n_samples, 1) rather than a 1D numpy array.

This could be a bit like https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/25370 where things get confused when pandas output is configured. I think the solution is different (TSNE's PCA is truely "internal only") but it seems like there might be something more general to investigate/think about related to pandas output and nested estimators. There is something quite smelly regarding the interaction between `IsotonicRegression` and pandas output: It seems that we output a pandas Series when calling `predict` which is something that we don't do for any other estimator. `IsotonicRegression` is already quite special since it accepts a single feature. I need to investigate more to understand why we wrap the output of the `predict` method. OK the reason is that `IsotonicRegression().predict(X)` call `IsotonicRegression().transform(X)` ;) I don't know if we should have: ```python def predict(self, T): with config_context(transform_output="default"): return self.transform(T) ``` or ```python def predict(self, T): return np.array(self.transform(T), copy=False).squeeze() ``` Another solution would be to have a private `_transform` function called by both `transform` and `predict`. In this way, the `predict` call will not call the wrapper that is around the public `transform` method. I think this is even cleaner than the previous code. /take

problem_statement.Title: ColumnTransformer + set_output('pandas') raises pandas Length mismatch when a transformer has an empty column selection
problem_statement.Description: When ColumnTransformer contains a step whose column selection is empty (e.g. ('categorical', 'passthrough', [])) and the pipeline has set_output(transform='pandas'), fitting the pipeline raises a pandas ValueError (Length mismatch) while ColumnTransformer is assembling the output DataFrame columns. The error occurs during ColumnTransformer._hstack when assigning DataFrame.columns.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect handling of empty 'passthrough' in ColumnTransformer._iter (sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py::ColumnTransformer._iter): the code replaces trans == 'passthrough' with a FunctionTransformer and executes it before checking _is_empty_column_selection(columns). This creates an Xs entry for an empty selection (wrong assumption: always create/run a passthrough FunctionTransformer).

2) Incorrect handling of empty 'passthrough' in ColumnTransformer._update_fitted_transformers (sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py::ColumnTransformer._update_fitted_transformers): the code checks old == 'passthrough' and calls next(fitted_transformers) before checking whether the column selection is empty. This assumes a fitted FunctionTransformer exists for every original 'passthrough' entry (wrong assumption: a fitted transformer is present even when the selection is empty).

3) Assumed alignment between produced Xs and iterator output in ColumnTransformer._hstack (sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py::ColumnTransformer._hstack): the method assumes the sequence produced by self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) (used to build transformer_names) aligns 1:1 and in order with the list Xs produced by _fit_transform. When _iter/_update_fitted_transformers behavior differs for empty selections, zip(transformer_names, feature_names_outs) becomes misaligned, and assigning output.columns = names_out triggers pandas' Length mismatch (wrong assumption: producer/consumer invariant holds despite creating/consuming passthroughs out of order).

4) Missing tests covering the combination (sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py test coverage): there are no existing unit tests exercising empty-selection passthrough together with set_output(transform='pandas'), so the control-flow/order bug in the iterator and updater was not caught (wrong assumption: existing tests exercise this code path).
StepsToReproduce: Create a pandas DataFrame X and an array y, e.g. X = pd.DataFrame([[1.0,2.0,3.0,4.0],[4,2,2,5]], columns=['a','b','c','d']); y = np.array([0,1])

Create a ColumnTransformer with a passthrough step for categorical_features and a transformer for numerical_features, e.g. ColumnTransformer([('categorical','passthrough', categorical_features), ('numerical', Pipeline([('scaler', RobustScaler()), ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median'))]), numerical_features)], remainder='drop')
Put this ColumnTransformer into a Pipeline and call .set_output(transform='pandas') on the Pipeline
Call pipeline.fit(X, y) and observe the ValueError
(Reference: the repro in the original report reproduces the failure.)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Fitting the pipeline completes without error. ColumnTransformer ignores transformer steps that select zero columns (they do not produce output columns), and the resulting pandas DataFrame returned by transform/fit_transform has column names that match the transformed features only.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pipeline.fit raises ValueError: Length mismatch (e.g. 'Expected axis has 3 elements, new values have 0 elements') coming from pandas when ColumnTransformer._hstack attempts to assign output.columns because the list of transformer names computed from the iterator is misaligned with the list of feature columns produced (traceback shows error during output.columns assignment).

problem_statement.Title: unique_labels raises ValueError for pandas nullable extension dtypes (Int64/Float64/boolean)
problem_statement.Description: Calling sklearn.utils.multiclass.unique_labels with pandas Series using nullable extension dtypes ("Int64", "Float64", "boolean") can raise ValueError("Mix type of y not allowed, got types {...}"), because those Series materialize as object-dtype arrays of pandas scalar/extension values and are misclassified as 'unknown'. The problem appears when type detection and label extraction treat object-dtype arrays containing pandas extension scalars or pd.NA as non-numeric/unknown.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sklearn/utils/multiclass.py::type_of_target — Incorrect object-dtype quick-return assumption: the code assumes "if not issparse(y) and y.dtype == object and not isinstance(y.flat[0], str): return 'unknown'". This encodes the wrong assumption that any non-string object-dtype first element implies unknown target types. It does not account for pandas nullable extension dtypes (Int64/Float64/boolean) which materialize as object arrays or pandas scalar objects (including pd.NA) but are numeric/boolean semantics. The correct relationship is: classification of object-dtype arrays must locate the first non-missing element (skip pd.NA) and classify based on that element's semantic type, not simply on y.dtype == object and the raw first element.

2) sklearn/utils/multiclass.py::_unique_multiclass — Wrong assumption about xp.asarray(y) results: _unique_multiclass assumes xp.asarray(y) yields a numeric/str numpy array suitable for xp.unique_values. When xp.asarray(y) returns an object-dtype array of pandas scalar/extension values (including pd.NA), unique_values returns an object array of pandas scalar types which can mix with other label types and cause downstream failures. The required relationship is that label extraction must treat pandas extension-backed object arrays as numeric/boolean arrays (coercing pd.NA -> np.nan) before calling unique/combination logic.

3) sklearn/utils/validation.py::check_array — Related (correct) behavior encoded elsewhere: check_array contains logic that detects pandas extension dtypes (using pandas.api.types.is_extension_array_dtype) and performs an early conversion (pd.Series(...).astype(...)) so pd.NA becomes np.nan and dtype becomes a numeric numpy dtype. The absence of equivalent conversion in type_of_target/_unique_multiclass causes inconsistent handling when code paths bypass check_array.

4) sklearn/utils/fixes.py::_object_dtype_isnan — Missing-value detection expectation: pd.NA should be treated as missing (detectable via X != X or pd.isna). Any scanning for a representative (first non-missing) element must skip pd.NA; otherwise an all-NA or leading-NA array may be misclassified.

(Each of the above is encoded in the listed file/function locations and contributes equally to the observed misclassification and label-extraction mismatch.)
StepsToReproduce: Install scikit-learn 1.2.1 and pandas with nullable dtypes support.
