problem_statement.Title: FeatureUnion + transform_output='pandas' raises ValueError when a transformer changes row count (aggregation)
problem_statement.Description: When sklearn.set_config(transform_output='pandas') is enabled, FeatureUnion (or other wrapped estimators) raises a ValueError if a child transformer returns fewer rows than the original input (e.g., groupby aggregation). The output wrapper unconditionally attempts to apply the original input's index to the transform result, causing a pandas Length mismatch error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Wrong assumption: The set_output wrapper assumes the transformer's output has the same number of rows as the original input. Location: sklearn/utils/_set_output.py::_wrap_in_pandas_container. Evidence: it unconditionally assigns the original input index via data_to_wrap.index = index and/or calls pd.DataFrame(..., index=index), which raises ValueError when len(data_to_wrap) != len(index).

2. Wrong forwarding of index: _wrap_data_with_container always forwards getattr(original_input, 'index', None) as the index parameter to _wrap_in_pandas_container without checking row-count correspondence. Location: sklearn/utils/_set_output.py::_wrap_data_with_container. Evidence: code calls _wrap_in_pandas_container(..., index=getattr(original_input, 'index', None), ...), and in the reproducer original_input.index length is 96 while transformed output length is 4.

3. FeatureUnion contribution: FeatureUnion._hstack returns pd.concat(child_outputs, axis=1) which can produce a DataFrame whose number of rows differs from original_input when child transformers perform aggregation. Location: sklearn/pipeline.py:FeatureUnion._hstack. Evidence: in the reproducer, pd.concat result length is 4 while original input length is 96; subsequent unconditional index override triggers the error.

4. Correct relationship (diagnostic): the wrapper should only override or pass the original input index when the output's row-count equals the original index length. Formally: only perform index assignment or pass index to pd.DataFrame when len(data_to_wrap) == len(index). If lengths differ, preserve data_to_wrap's own index. This relationship is required because the current hardcoded behavior (always assigning index) is only correct under the assumption of identical row counts.

5. Remaining gap (potential secondary error): the investigation did not verify that estimator.get_feature_names_out matches the number of columns after aggregation/concatenation. A columns-length mismatch could still raise later when columns are assigned; this is a separate validation concern.
StepsToReproduce: Create an hourly pandas DataFrame from 2020-01-01 to 2020-01-05 (exclusive) with a 'value' column and a 'date' column derived from the index (date only), producing 96 rows.
Define a transformer whose transform returns X['value'].groupby(X['date']).sum() (an aggregation returning 4 rows / one per day).
Wrap the transformer in a FeatureUnion: make_union(MyTransformer()).
Call fit_transform(data) on the FeatureUnion and observe the failure.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No exception is raised. The FeatureUnion.fit_transform call returns a pandas object (DataFrame/Series) representing the transformed output; its index reflects the transformer's actual output (the aggregated index of length 4 in the reproducer) and the result contains the aggregated values. The wrapper should not force the original input's index onto outputs with different row counts.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A ValueError is raised: "Length mismatch: Expected axis has 4 elements, new values have 96 elements" (pandas) originating from sklearn/utils/_set_output.py when the wrapper attempts to assign the original input index (length 96) to the transformed DataFrame (length 4). The failure occurs only when transform_output='pandas' and not when the default (numpy) output is used.

hints_text:
As noted in the [glossery](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/glossary.html#term-transform), Scikit-learn transformers expects that `transform`'s output have the same number of samples as the input. This exception is held in `FeatureUnion` when processing data and tries to make sure that the output index is the same as the input index. In principle, we can have a less restrictive requirement and only set the index if it is not defined.

To better understand your use case, how do you intend to use the `FeatureUnion` in the overall pipeline?


> Scikit-learn transformers expects that transform's output have the same number of samples as the input

I haven't known that. Good to know. What is the correct way to aggregate or drop rows in a pipeline? Isn't that supported?

> To better understand your use case, how do you intend to use the FeatureUnion in the overall pipeline?

The actual use case: I have a time series (`price`) with hourly frequency. It is a single series with a datetime index. I have built a dataframe with pipeline and custom transformers (by also violating the rule to have same number of inputs and outputs) which aggregates the data (calculates daily mean, and some moving average of daily means) then I have transformed back to hourly frequency (using same values for all the hours of a day). So the dataframe has (`date`, `price`, `mean`, `moving_avg`) columns at that point with hourly frequency ("same number input/output" rule violated again). After that I have added the problematic `FeatureUnion`. One part of the union simply drops `price` and "collapses" the remaining part to daily data (as I said all the remaining columns has the same values on the same day). On the other part of the feature union I calculate a standard devition between `price` and `moving_avg` on daily basis. So I have the (`date`, `mean`, `moving_avg`) on the left side of the feature union and an `std` on the right side. Both have daily frequency. I would like to have a dataframe with (`date`, `mean`, `moving_avg`, `std`) at the end of the transformation.
As I see there is the same "problem" in `ColumnTransfromer`.
I have a look at how `scikit-learn` encapsulates output into a `DataFrame` and found this code block:

https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/utils/_set_output.py#L55-L62

Is there any reason to set index here? If transformer returned a `DataFrame` this already has some kind of index. Why should we restore the original input index? What is the use case when a transformer changes the `DataFrame`'s index and `scikit-learn` has to restore it automatically to the input index?

With index restoration it is also expected for transformers that index should not be changed (or if it is changed by transformer then `scikit-learn` restores the original one which could be a bit unintuitive). Is this an intended behaviour?

What is the design decision to not allow changing index and row count in data by transformers? In time series problems I think it is very common to aggregate raw data and modify original index.
